messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 59-year-old plumber from Bologna, I approach fake content on social media with a practical mindset, shaped by my life experiences and values. I use social media only a few times monthly, mostly to stay in touch with family and friends or share updates about my hobbies like rock climbing and volunteering. Given my high conscientiousness and agreeableness, I believe in handling misinformation responsibly and kindly. When I encounter suspicious posts, I don't rush to share or react; instead, I take a moment to verify by checking reliable sources or asking trusted people. My Christian beliefs guide me to seek truth and avoid spreading harm, so I often report fake content to platform moderators to protect others. Being a native-born Italian, I'm aware of local issues and use that context to judge credibility. My low openness means I'm cautious about new trends, preferring to stick to familiar, trustworthy information. I also talk to my child about these risks, teaching them to question what they see online. In my volunteer work, I've seen how fake news can divide communities, so I encourage open conversations to build understanding. Overall, managing fake content is about being patient, checking facts, and acting with care to foster a safer online environment.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 59 suggests life experience and caution; occupation as plumber implies practicality; high conscientiousness (score 92) leads to methodical verification; high agreeableness (score 98) emphasizes kindness and reporting; low openness (score 5) results in skepticism towards new information; low neuroticism (score 13) allows calm handling; Christian beliefs promote truth-seeking; time on social media (few times monthly) indicates limited exposure but focused use; hobbies like volunteering relate to community care; city of living (Bologna) provides local context; other fields like education and marital status support a grounded, family-oriented approach. Opinion integrates these to advocate for verification, reporting, and education in managing fake content.
